coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent turn-on carbon dot-cored pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles for tumor-specific dual-triggered drug delivery
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Pei, Mingliang
Zhao, Xiaomei
Li, Guoping
Chen, Wei
Liu, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Polymer prodrugs have attracted intense attention in tumor chemotherapy in the form of nanoparticles, nanogels or micelles. However, it is still a challenge to control their particle size and stability. Here, carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer hybrid prodrug (CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG) has been designed for the tumor-specific fluorescent turn-on and pH/reduction dual-triggered drug delivery. The fluorescence of CDs was almost completely quenched after the surface modification and drug conjugation via both static and dynamic quenching effects. It could easily self-assemble into pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles in aqueous system with CDs as fluorescent off-on core, acid-labile polymer prodrug block as shell and reduction-triggered detached PEG block as corona, possessing excellent tumor-specific pH and reduction dual-triggered drug release performance. Furthermore, the fluorescence of CDs could be partially recovered after the drug release because of the relief of the dynamic quenching during the drug release.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles
Core-shell-corona structure
Carbon dots
Dual-triggered drug release
Fluorescent traceable

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer hybrid prodrug (CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province , China (Grant No. 18JR3RA271 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CDs (a), CD-PMHy-ssPEG (b) and CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG (c).
Hydrodynamic diameters of CDs (a) and CD-PMMA-ssPEG, CD-PMHy-ssPEG and CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG (b).

UV–vis spectra of CDs, DOX, CD-PMHy-ssPEG and CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG in aqueous solution (a) and FT-IR spectra of CD-PMMA-Br (A), CD-PMMA-ssPEG (B), CD-PMHy-ssPEG (C), and CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG (D) by KBr pellet (b).

PL emission spectra of (a) CDs (0.01 mg/mL), (b) CD-PMHy-ssPEG (0.60 mg/mL) and (c) CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG (0.60 mg/mL) in water system under the different excitation wavelength, inset images of CDs and CD-PMHy-ssPEG in aqueous solution under daylight irradiation and UV irradiation at 365 nm, respectively.
1H NMR spectra of CDs, CD-Br, CD-PMMA-Br, CD-PMMA-ssPEG and CD-PMHy-ssPEG.
In vitro release profiles of DOX from the CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles at different media at 37 ℃ (mean ± SD, n = 3).
Synthesis route for the carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer hybrid prodrug (CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG).
Fitted DOX release parameters with the Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent turn-on carbon dot-cored pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles for tumor-specific dual-triggered drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymer prodrugs have attracted intense attention in tumor chemotherapy in the form of nanoparticles, nanogels or micelles. However, it is still a challenge to control their particle size and stability. Here, carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer hybrid prodrug (CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG) has been designed for the tumor-specific fluorescent turn-on and pH/reduction dual-triggered drug delivery. The fluorescence of CDs was almost completely quenched after the surface modification and drug conjugation via both static and dynamic quenching effects. It could easily self-assemble into pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles in aqueous system with CDs as fluorescent off-on core, acid-labile polymer prodrug block as shell and reduction-triggered detached PEG block as corona, possessing excellent tumor-specific pH and reduction dual-triggered drug release performance. Furthermore, the fluorescence of CDs could be partially recovered after the drug release because of the relief of the dynamic quenching during the drug release.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer prodrugs have been investigated in tumor chemotherapy [1], owing to their desired drug release performance originated from the tumor-specific conjugation via pH-labile [2] or redox-cleavable [3] dynamic covalent bonds. They have been usually formulated into nanomedicine, such as nanoparticles, nanogels or micelles, in order to achieve the passive targeting via the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect [4].

The polymer prodrug-based anti-tumor nanomedicines are mainly fabricated by the following two approaches: i) conjugating drug onto the ready-made polymer nanoparticles or nanogels as drug carriers [5]; or ii) self-assembly of the copolymer prodrugs into nanoparticles [6] or micelles [7], determined by the hydrophilic-hydrophobic structure of the copolymers. Unfortunately, it is usually hard to control the diameter and distribution of the polymer prodrug nanoparticles for both above methods. Relatively, with a suitable hydrophilic-hydrophobic ratio, the polymer prodrug micelles could be obtained with unique diameter and narrow distribution [8]. However, their stability is still a challenge due to the demicellization upon dilution during intravenous administration. So, an additional crosslinking process is needed for the self-assembled polymer prodrug micelles, in order to improve their stability [9].

Recently, unimolecular micelles have been developed for drug delivery, because of their excellent stability in comparison with the conventional supramolecular micelles [10]. They can maintain structural stability after being subjected to extreme surrounding environment changes such as high dilution, due to unique single molecular architectures. They have been widely designed for drug delivery [11]. The unimolecular micelles have been usually fabricated with star-like copolymers containing cyclodextrin [12–14], polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimer [15–18], or hyperbranched polymer [19–21] as core. To simplify the preparation process and introduce new function, inorganic nanoparticles, such as polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane (POSS) [22] and magnetite nanoparticles [23], have been used as cores for the pseudo unimolecular micelle-based drug nanocarriers for non-covalent drug-loading. By now, there is no report on the pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles with functional inorganic nanoparticles as core.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been used for various applications owing to their excellent tunable photoluminescence and photo-stability [24], especially for biomedical imaging. However, in most of the reported CDs-based drug delivery systems (DDSs), there was only fluorescence intensity change during the drug release, which is also affected with their concentration. This severely limits their practical application for fluorescence imaging in cancer diagnosis. In the present work, CDs have been used as core in the star-like block copolymer, by the surface initiated-atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP) of methyl methacrylate (MMA) from the CDs-based macroinitiators, followed with postpolymerization PEGylation and drug conjugation ( Scheme 1). The fluorescence-quenched CD-cored star-like copolymer could easily self-assemble into core-shell-corona pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles for tumor-specific dual-triggered drug delivery, in which the CD was the fluorescent turn-on core for imaging, the hydrophobic prodrug block via acid-labile conjugation was the shell for pH-triggered drug release, whereas the surface hydrophilic PEG brush was the bioreducible detached corona to prolong the cycling life. Most importantly, the fluorescence could be turned on for imaging application during drug release.

The CDs-based macroinitiators (CD-Br) were prepared by functionalization of CDs with 2-bromoisobutyric acid (BiBA) [25]. PEG-Cy was synthesized by the amidation of the carboxyl of PEG-COOH (Mn = 2 kDa) with cystamine (Cy) [26]. Propiolic acid functionalized PEG (PC-ssPEG) was synthesized by amidation of PEG-Cy with propiolic acid ( Scheme 2). 1H NMR (CDCI3, δ, ppm) 8.11 (-NHCO-), 6.90 (-NH-), 3.64 (PEG, -CH2CH2O-), 3.51 (PEG, CH 3CH2O-), 4.19 (-OCOCH 2-), 2.88 (-CH, -SCH 2-) (Fig. S1).

As shown in Scheme 1, the carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer hybrid prodrug (CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG) were designed by the SI-ATRP of MMA from the CD-Br macroinitiators, followed with PEGylation via click reaction, and acid-labile drug conjugation. For the SI-ATRP, 43.86 mg of CD-Br, 10.44 µL (0.05 mmol) of N,N,N′,N",N"-pentamethyldiethylenetriamine (PMDETA), 420 µL MMA were dissolved in anhydrous DMF in a dry Schlenk tube. Then the mixture undergone three-pump-thaw cycles under N2 atmosphere. After adding CuBr (11.16 mg, 0.11 mmol), the polymerization was carried out at 80 ℃ for 8 h under nitrogen. The product, CD-PMMA-Br, was collected by thorough dialyzing (MWCO of 3500) against deionized water and freeze-drying.

The CD-PMMA-N3 was synthesized by a simple nucleophilic substitution of the bromine-terminal CD-PMMA-Br, as following: CD-PMMA-Br (83.86 g) was dispersed into 10.0 mL of DMF. Then NaN3 (16.25 mg, 0.25 mmol) was added and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The product was collected by thorough dialyzing (MWCO of 3500) against deionized water and freeze-drying.

Subsequently, the CD-PMMA-N3 was PEGylated with PC-ssPEG via click reaction. CD-PMMA-N3 (43.86 mg) and PC-ssPEG (0.15 g) were dissolved into 6.0 mL of DMF. After CuBr (0.016 g) and PMDETA (10 μL) were added, the mixture was degassed with three cycles of freeze-pump-thaw processes, refilled with N2 gas, and heated at 80 ℃ for 8 h. The product, CD-PMMA-ssPEG, was collected by thorough dialyzing (MWCO of 3500) against deionized water and freeze-drying.

Finally, DOX was conjugated onto the carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer after functionalization with hydrazine. CD-PMMA-ssPEG (43.86 mg) and hydrazine hydrate (1 mL) were dissolved in DMF (15 mL), and mixture was stirred at room temperature for 12 h. The resultant CD-PMHy-ssPEG was collected by thorough dialyzing (MWCO of 3500) against deionized water and freeze-drying. Then, CD-PMHy-ssPEG (40.0 mg) was dispersed in 20 mL of DMF containing 20.0 mg of DOX. After 10 µL of trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) and 200 µL of triethylamine (TEA) were added. The mixture was bubbled with N2 gas for 30 min and then stirred at room temperature for 24 h in dark. The carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer hybrid prodrug (CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG) was collected by thorough dialyzing (MWCO of 3500) against deionized water and freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer hybrid prodrug (CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG)
ce:section.ce:para: To design the carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer hybrid prodrug (CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG), the PMMA brushes were grafted from the CD-based macro-initiator via the SI-ATRP of MMA, for DOX conjugation via acid-labile hydrazone bond after transformation the methyl ester groups into hydrazide groups. Furthermore, the hydrophilic PEG blocks were modified onto the carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer from the C-Br active end groups via a facile click reaction, as a reduction-detachable corona, as shown in Scheme 1.

The amino-riched fluorescent CDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine [25]. They showed a particle size of 2–3 nm in the TEM observation ( Fig. 1a) and a mean hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) of 3.5 nm in the DLS analysis ( Fig. 2a). The Dh was slightly higher than the particle size in the TEM result because of swelling. The brown curve in the UV absorption diagram shows two absorption peaks originated from the carbogenic core and surface defects of the carbon dots: the one at 238 nm due to the π → π* transition of C˭C and the one at around 352 nm of the C‒N and C˭O transitions (both n → π*) respectively ( Fig. 3a) [27]. In addition, the fluorescence spectra indicated that a strong excitation-independent blue fluorescence was achieved at excitation wavelength around 350 nm ( Fig. 4a), making them promising potential for real-time fluorescence imaging in traceable nanomedicine.

The CD-Br was synthesized by amidation of the amino groups and esterification of the hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CDs with excessive 2-bromoisobutyric acid (BiBA). Compared with the 1H NMR spectrum of the pristine CDs, a new signal at chemical shift of 1.58 ppm could be seen in the CD-Br, ascribed to the proton a ( Fig. 5) [28], indicating that successful preparation of the macroinitiator CD-Br. Then the PMMA brushes were introduced onto the CDs via the SI-ATRP of MMA. The characteristic chemical shift of the proton b in PMMA appeared at 0.74 ppm [29], demonstrating the successful SI-ATRP. Moreover, the integral area ratio of the protons a and b was 6:16.83, meaning a polymerization degree of MMA of about 6 in the PMMA brushes.

The resulted bromine-terminal CD-PMMA-Br was then transformed into CD-PMMA-N3 by a simple nucleophilic substitution for the facile PEGylation via click reaction. Here, PC-ssPEG was synthesized as a PEGylation agent (Scheme 2), in which the bioreducible disulfide bond was used to achieve the reduction-triggered detaching. After the click reaction, the characteristic absorbance of C‒O‒C in PEG appeared at 1105 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of the product (Fig. 3b). Furthermore, the proton signal in Cy fragment appeared at 2.72 ppm whereas the one around 3.50 ppm increased due to the introduction of PEG chains (Fig. 5). All the results revealed the successful PEGylation. The CD-PMMA-ssPEG showed a Dh of 212 nm with a vary narrow distribution in aqueous system, in which the PMMA blocks are insoluble whereas the PEG blocks are soluble (Fig. 2b). As dispersed into DMF in which both blocks could be dissolved, a slightly higher Dh of 248 nm was obtained (Fig. S2a). The results indicated that the carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer could self-assemble into pseudo unimolecular micelles in the aqueous system.

To conjugate DOX via the acid-labile hydrazone bond, hydrazine has been modified onto the PMMA blocks. It could be revealed by the FT-IR and 1H NMR analysis. The characteristic absorbance of C˭O in amide groups appeared at 1635 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of the product (Fig. 3b). The integral area of the protons at the chemical shift of around 3.50 ppm decreased from 544.88 to 528.13 after the modification (Fig. 5). The resultant CD-PMHy-ssPEG showed a particle size of about 50 nm in the TEM analysis (Fig. 1b). The DLS results showed a unimodal normal hydrodynamic diameter distribution in 350–590 nm in water while 92–650 nm in DMF, meaning the pseudo unimolecular micelles in water.

Finally, DOX was conjugated onto the CD-PMHy-ssPEG through the formation of acid-labile hydrazone bond. After that, the characteristic absorbance of C˭N bond could be seen at 1590 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum, as well as the characteristic absorbance of DOX at 1153 and 898 cm−1 (Fig. 3b), demonstrating the successful conjugation of DOX via the hydrazone bond. The DOX content was determined as 33.78% via UV–vis analysis after complete disintegration in an extremely acidic medium [30].

The particle size of the carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer hybrid prodrug (CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG) increased to around 100 nm from 50 nm of the CD-PMHy-ssPEG because of the drug conjugation with a high DOX content (Fig. 1c). The CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG seemed aggregates with each other, maybe due to the intermolecular interactions, such as hydrophobic interaction and π-π interaction, between the hydrophobic blocks during the drying procedure. The Dh of the CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG was measured as 350 nm, smaller than the CD-PMHy-ssPEG of 444 nm (Fig. 2b), also due to the DOX conjugation. In the CD-PMHy-ssPEG pseudo unimolecular micelles, both the PMHy shell and PEG corona are highly swollen because of their solubility in water. After DOX conjugation, the mediate shell became hydrophobic in the final pseudo unimolecular CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG prodrug micelles. Therefore, the particle size increased but the Dh decreased with DOX conjugation.

The unique particle in TEM analysis and the unimodal normal hydrodynamic diameter distribution in the DLS result demonstrated the self-assembly of the carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer hybrid prodrug into pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles in water system, with the CD as core, hydrophobic prodrug blocks as shell, and hydrophilic PEG brushes as corona, respectively. With the unique structure, the hydrodynamic diameter of the pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles could be controlled by adjusting the length and grafting density of the PMHyDOX and PEG blocks in the carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer hybrid prodrug.

The absorption at 352 nm of the CD-PMHy-ssPEG became weak in comparison with the CDs (Fig. 3a), because of the static quenching effect via the amidation of the surface amino groups on CDs [31]. Even though, a strong blue fluorescence could still be seen with excitation at 365 nm UV (Fig. 4b), meaning a potential application for fluorescent imaging.

After the DOX conjugation, the fluorescence of the final CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG was almost completely quenched, due to the additional dynamic quenching effect by the interaction between the CD cores with the surrounding conjugated DOX molecules in the prodrug blocks [31]. The dynamic quenching effect could be revealed by the UV absorption spectrum (Fig. 3a), in which the characteristic absorption of CDs at 352 nm became very weak, and the one of DOX at 480 nm did not appear whereas a new one appeared at 554 nm. The red shift revealed the interaction between the CDs and the surrounding conjugated DOX molecules, which caused the dynamic quenching effect.

As carbon dot-cored pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles, the CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG did not emit fluorescence, however, the dynamic quenching effect of the surrounding conjugated DOX molecules would be relieved during the drug release in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment, by cleaving the acid-labile hydrazone conjugation. As a result, the carbon dot-cored pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles transformed into CD-PMHy-ssPEG, which could emit strong fluorescence, indicating a turn-on fluorescence triggered by the drug release in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment. It indicated that the fluorescence of the proposed carbon dot-cored pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles could only be achieved in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment, meaning an expected potential application for real-time monitoring the drug release behavior and imaging diagnosis in tumor sites.

The drug release behavior of the proposed pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles was investigated in different media at 37 °C. As shown in Fig. 6, there was no burst release for all cases. The release ratio was only 7.06% at pH 7.4 PBS, much lower than that of 28.46% at pH 5.0 ABS, indicating the pH-triggered drug release by cleaving the acid-labile hydrazone conjugation. To simulate the premature drug leakage in the normal physiological media and the desired tumor-specific drug release in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment, the drug release was also conducted in the pH 7.4 PBS + 10 μM GSH and pH 5.0 ABS + 10 mM GSH, respectively. With the low reductant level in the normal physiological media, the premature drug leakage was 8.96% within 51.5 h in the medium of pH 7.4 PBS + 10 μM GSH. As for the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment with a high GSH concentration, the cumulative release increased to 67.97% in a sustained release mode. Furthermore, the drug release rate was much higher in the acidic media with 10 mM GSH than that in the acidic media without GSH. It demonstrated the reduction-triggered drug release by detaching the PEG brushes via cleaving the bioreducible disulfide bond, which would accelerate the diffusion of the H+ ions into the prodrug block shell to cleave the hydrazone conjugation, as well as the diffusion of the released DOX into the releasing media.

To further understand the release mechanism from the proposed pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles, the drug release profiles were plotted with the Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models (Fig. S3). The main parameters were summarized in Table 1. All the correlation coefficient (R2) obtained with the Higuchi equation were higher than those with the Korsmeyer-Peppas model, indicating the drug release could be better explained with the Higuchi kinetic model, namely Fickian diffusion mechanism [32]. Furthermore, the kinetic constant (k) increased with increasing the acidity or GSH concentration in the releasing media, also demonstrating the pH/reduction dual-triggered drug release performance.

In summary, carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer hybrid prodrug (CD-PMHyDOX-ssPEG) has been designed for the tumor-specific turn-on fluorescence and pH/reduction dual-triggered drug delivery. The static quenching effect introduced by the static quenching effect due to the surface modification and the dynamic quenching effect caused by the surrounding conjugated DOX molecules completely quenched the fluorescence of CDs. The pseudo unimolecular micelles could be easily obtained by dispersing the proposed carbon dot-cored star-like copolymer hybrid prodrug into water, with CDs as fluorescent off-on core, acid-labile polymer prodrug block as shell and reduction-triggered detachable PEG block as corona. The pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles possessed excellent tumor-specific pH/reduction dual-triggered drug release performance, by cleaving the acid-labile hydrazone conjugates and the bioreducible disulfide linkages in the simulated tumor intracellular microenvironment. Moreover, the dynamic quenching effect of the conjugated DOX could be relieved during the drug release, and the turn-on fluorescence of CDs could be used for the real-time fluorescence imaging diagnosis and monitoring the drug release in tumor sites. In the form of pseudo unimolecular prodrug micelles with CD cores, higher stability could be achieved in comparison with the conventional micelles by self-assembly of the polymer prodrugs, besides the introduction of fluorescence imaging function. And the diameter is expected to be controlled by adjusting the grafting density and length of the prodrug blocks. Such features make the proposed strategy a versatile candidate for future tumor treatment.

Mingliang Pei: Investigation, Data curation. Xiaomei Zhao: Investigation. Guoping Li: Investigation. Wei Chen: Investigation. Peng Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (Grant No. 18JR3RA271).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.126490.

ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
